petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented individual seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, specifically as a registered somatic movement therapist. She began her career as a dancer and is certified in movement techniques and massage, including the Feldenkrais technique, Body-Mind Centering, and aquatic therapy (obtaining her first certification in movement therapy in 1993). She had previously been approved for nonimmigrant visas in a similar classification. The director and AAO determined her field was more properly classified as recreational therapy rather than a distinct science, since she is not a licensed physical therapist.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A 1982 travel and study grant from the Committee on Arts and Arts Education in Brussels, which the petitioner asserts was to study dance.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that scholarships funding training in one's field are not awards that can meet this criterion, because the most experienced members of the field do not compete for such scholarships. Additionally, the scholarship was for studying dance, not movement therapy, and was not issued in recognition of excellence as a movement therapist. The petitioner did not obtain certification in movement therapy until 1993. A 1982 scholarship to study dance is not indicative of sustained national or international acclaim as a movement therapist 20 years later at the time of filing. The petitioner did not directly contest the director's conclusion on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Certificates affirming training in various movement therapy techniques, and evidence of membership in the Association for Prenatal and Perinatal Psychology and Health (APPPAH). In response to the RFE, the petitioner submitted certification requirements for her credentials but no materials regarding APPPAH membership requirements.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that certification awarded upon completion of specified training is not a membership. Even assuming the certifying organizations had prestige, a license to practice in one's field is only a criterion for the lesser 'aliens of exceptional ability' classification under section 203(b)(2), not for extraordinary ability. Regarding APPPAH membership, the record did not establish that the association requires outstanding achievements of its members. The appeal exhibits did not address this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: An unsigned letter confirming the petitioner's appearance on 'Everybody's Angels' on TCI Cable Channel 27 (airing only in San Francisco); evidence of interviews on KUSF-FM radio of the University of San Francisco on at least three occasions; promotional materials for seminars led by the petitioner on Body-Mind Centering; materials about the Lar Lubovitch Dance Troupe (none mentioning the petitioner by name); and foreign dance reviews, some mentioning the petitioner by name but not primarily about her.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concluded the cable and radio broadcasts were limited to the San Francisco area and could not be considered major media, as they did not provide national exposure. Promotional materials, advertisements, and press releases are not as persuasive as independent journalistic coverage. The dance reviews were not primarily about the petitioner and did not relate to the field of expertise claimed (movement therapy). In response to the director's RFE requesting circulation data, the petitioner no longer claimed to meet this criterion, and the appeal did not address it.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: A letter from Bonnie Bainbridge Cohen, founder of Body-Mind Centering, asserting the petitioner has taught this technique; an invitation to participate in a health forum (with no evidence the forum took place prior to filing); and a letter from Bobbi Jo Lyman, Editor-in-Chief for the Journal of Prenatal and Perinatal Psychology and Health (published by APPPAH), stating that people with certain specializations are invited to be part of the peer review process and asserting the petitioner is a member of the Editorial Board.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that evaluating one's students is inherent to teaching and does not establish national or international acclaim. Demonstrating or discussing one's technique at a symposium does not involve judging the work of others. The petitioner did not establish the symposium occurred prior to the filing date, citing Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45, 49 (Comm. 1971). The journal's letterhead listed three editors and an associate editor, none of whom was the petitioner; rather, the 'editorial board' was simply the pool of peer-reviewers. While serving as a peer-reviewer for a peer-reviewed medical journal could serve as minimal evidence, Lyman's letter did not identify which manuscripts the petitioner reviewed or whether these reviews occurred prior to the filing date.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted her experience constituted an original contribution and cited her certification in Feldenkrais, Body-Mind Centering, and aquatic therapy (noting 2,000 practitioners of Feldenkrais exist). Letters from institutions and families of children she had treated attested to her unique training in both Feldenkrais and Body-Mind Centering. A letter from Martha Eddy, President of the International Somatic Movement Education and Therapy Association, stated the petitioner's integration of these two methods 'may one day be recognized as a milestone in our field.' A letter from the founder of the Physical Therapy Center noted her methods 'are an ideal supplement to the traditional physical therapy and rehabilitation programs.' A letter from Susan Stein (marriage and family therapist and dance student) praised her 'Intimate Embrace Tango' approach as integrating movement therapy with tango instruction. Additional references asserted the petitioner produced results for children with cerebral palsy and autism. The petitioner also submitted 'The Feldenkrais Method' by Mark Reese, and on appeal an article from a journal on Pain Management investigating the Feldenkrais method benefits, plus a letter from a child psychologist about alternative and complimentary medical therapies and letters from a physician about patient improvements.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that not every combination of training can be considered a contribution of major significance. Martha Eddy's speculation that the integration 'may one day' be recognized was insufficient. The Physical Therapy Center letter did not indicate any accredited university considered integrating the petitioner's techniques into physical therapy curricula. Susan Stein's single testimonial did not establish that integrating movement therapy with dance instruction constitutes a contribution of major significance, and the record lacked evidence that other dance instructors had adopted the petitioner's techniques. Testimonials from satisfied clients are not evidence of impact on the field. The AAO rejected the argument that somatic movement therapy constitutes complimentary medicine as a separate science, citing Fontanarosa PB, Lundberg GD, 'Alternative medicine meets science,' JAMA 280:1618-1619 (1998), noting there is only evidence-based medicine or unproven medicine. The Feldenkrais Method document itself stated Functional Integration 'is neither medical nor therapeutic as a methodology.' The Pain Management article admitted its study was neither controlled nor blind and attributed benefits potentially to a placebo effect. Regardless, the petitioner did not develop the Feldenkrais method, and no researchers were investigating the petitioner's integration of Feldenkrais with Body-Mind Centering. The glowing Body-Mind Centering evaluations came from the technique's founder and the president of the institution promoting it. The child psychologist's letter did not explain how the petitioner's methods were superior to others, and the physician's letter did not explain whether results were atypical for movement therapy generally. Citing Walters v. Metro. Educ. Enters., 519 U.S. 202, 209 (1997) and Bailey v. U.S., 516 U.S. 137, 145 (1995), the AAO noted 'of major significance' must have meaning, and the record lacked evidence the petitioner impacted the field as a whole.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed leading or critical roles for the Bridge School (with a letter from its Executive Director confirming her position as a lead consultant in movement therapy and materials establishing the school's distinguished national reputation), the Institute of Holistic Healing Studies at San Francisco State University (with a letter from Kenn Burrows inviting her to speak again based on positive feedback), the Feldenkrais Guild of North America (with a letter from the Assistant Director stating she presented her Intimate Embrace Tango techniques at a conference), and the Physical Therapy Center (with a letter from the founder affirming intent to represent her for seminars, lectures, and training sessions in California, Michigan, and across the United States, and estimating her earnings). The petitioner also claimed a critical role for the Women's Building in San Francisco through a letter from Jovida Ross, Community Resource Coordinator, stating the petitioner was performing treatment sessions for low-income women and was an invited presenter for the Women's Resource Fair (scheduled after the filing date).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that while the Bridge School enjoyed a distinguished reputation, the role of lead consultant was not established as a leading or critical role; the petitioner did not even claim to have worked directly for the school on her Form G-325A, and the record lacked evidence of how many consultants the school utilized or how often her services were used. Although the school disseminates significant developments, there was no evidence it disseminated a summary of the petitioner's work. For the Institute for Holistic Healing Studies, although it had sponsored her for nonimmigrant visas, no evidence showed she actually worked there. For the Feldenkrais Guild, the record did not establish she worked for the guild, and it was unclear the guild enjoyed a distinguished national reputation. For the Physical Therapy Center, no evidence confirmed employment or actual income. Presenting one's work at a conference is not playing a leading or critical role for an entity, which requires a more long-term relationship. The record lacked evidence that the other entities enjoyed a distinguished national reputation.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the record did not establish that the petitioner has distinguished herself as a recreational or movement therapist to such an extent that she may be said to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of her field. While the evidence indicated the petitioner shows talent as a recreational or movement therapist, it was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field. The petitioner failed to meet at least three of the regulatory criteria, and the AAO also questioned whether somatic movement therapy constituted its own field or science, noting she was not a licensed physical therapist. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and she did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Walters v. Metro. Educ. Enters., 519 U.S. 202, 209 (1997)
Fontanarosa PB, Lundberg GD, 'Alternative medicine meets science,' Journal of the American Medical Association 280:1618-1619, 1998
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner asserted on appeal that the director's RFE simply 'regurgitated' the regulatory requirements without requesting specific evidence, but the AAO rejected this characterization, noting the RFE requested specific evidence such as the minimum requirements and criteria for association membership. The AAO also noted that prior approval of nonimmigrant visas in a similar classification does not mandate approval of an immigrant visa, as each case must be decided on its own evidence. Additionally, the petitioner failed to establish that several events and activities (health forum, symposium presentation, Women's Resource Fair) occurred prior to the filing date, as required by Matter of Katigbak.
